original_abstract: Recent advancements in membrane material science have expanded the demand for innovative, highly specialized separation technologies that offer superior efficiency and chemical resistance. A particularly vital application emerging in this field is the selective separation of rare earth elements (REEs). Given their critical role in modern industry and the rapidly growing demand for raw materials, the isolation of REEs has been identified as one of the seven chemical separation processes whose optimization would yield significant global benefits. Membrane-based strategies have gained momentum in metal recovery and wastewater treatment as sustainable, "green" alternatives characterized by operational simplicity. While various methodologies for REE separation have been developed, the current literature lacks comprehensive syntheses of these advancements. Specifically, there is an urgent need to focus on the transition from liquid to non-liquid membranes. Traditional liquid membranes often suffer from poor long-term stability and inconsistent extraction yields, prompting a shift toward more durable, solid-state membrane materials. This review evaluates the progress and implementation of these non-liquid strategies, highlighting their potential to overcome the limitations of earlier technologies.
humanized_abstract: New membrane materials require innovative, tailored, efficient and chemically stable separation processes. The specific separation of rare earth elements (REEs) is a newly emerging area of membrane technology and resources demand. The optimization of the 7 chemical separation processes including REEs extraction has been identified as a priority area with high global impact. Membrane technology, as a simple, sustainable “green” technology has been successfully demonstrated in metal separation and wastewater treatment processes. However, a clear overview of the literature concerning REEs extraction and membrane processes is not yet available. There is an urgent need to advance liquid membranes to solid (non-liquid) membranes, simply because liquid membranes are plagued with poor stability, heterogeneous extraction performance etc. This article provides an overview of the non-liquid membranes for REEs separation.

The optimization of the 7 chemical separation processes including REEs extraction has been identified as a priority area with high global impact. Membrane technology, as a simple, sustainable “green” technology has been successfully demonstrated in metal separation and wastewater treatment processes. However, a clear overview of the literature concerning REEs extraction and membrane processes is not yet available. There is an urgent need to advance liquid membranes to solid (non-liquid) membranes, simply because liquid membranes are plagued with poor stability, heterogeneous extraction performance etc. This article provides an overview of the non-liquid membranes for REEs separation.
